ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311069); no specific oat-honey-milk-tea entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific milk-tea-with-oat-and-honey facet exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to oat honey milk tea. No suitable parent concept identified for this commercial beverage category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of oat honey milk tea, approximately 500 ml (500 g), as typically served at a Taiwanese-style tea shop in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea (with sugar and whole milk), estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for brewed black tea, whole milk, honey, and oat milk; cross-referenced with NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic milk tea values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; NCC Food and Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for oat honey milk tea as a composite beverage. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries: brewed black tea (FDC 171917), whole milk (FDC 746782), honey (FDC 169640), and oat milk (FDC 719016), combined in proportions typical of a Taiwanese-style milk tea (approximately 40% brewed tea, 30% dairy milk or creamer, 20% oat milk, 10% honey by weight). Energy and macros are cross-checked against NCC generic milk-tea category values. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across vendors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based tea beverages and honey; analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for milk tea and honey
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for oat honey milk tea. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries: sweetened milk tea GI approximately 50–60 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); honey GI approximately 55–58 (Foster-Powell et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr). Oat milk component has a GI of approximately 69 (University of Sydney GI Database, oat milk entry), but its diluted proportion in the final beverage moderates the composite GI. Weighted composite estimate yields GI ≈ 55. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 56.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 30.8.

hypertension.guidance: Oat honey milk tea contains approximately 140 mg sodium per 500 ml serving, which is low relative to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily beverage sugar intake, as high sugar consumption can indirectly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content and medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 500 ml serving of oat honey milk tea delivers approximately 49 g of sugar and a glycemic load of approximately 31, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption and request reduced-sugar or sugar-free versions where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and saturated fat from dairy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high added-sugar content from honey and the saturated fat contributed by dairy milk or creamer in oat honey milk tea are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and opt for lower-fat, lower-sugar formulations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oat Honey Milk Tea (燕麦蜂蜜奶茶) is a Taiwanese-style milk tea beverage sold in Singapore, notably by CHICHA San Chen at One Fullerton. It is prepared by combining freshly brewed black tea with oat milk, dairy milk or creamer, and honey as the primary sweetener, then shaken or blended to achieve a smooth, lightly frothy texture. The drink has a mildly earthy, creamy flavour profile with floral sweetness from the honey and a subtle oat undertone that distinguishes it from conventional milk teas. It is served chilled or at room temperature in a medium cup of approximately 500 ml. Nutritionally, a single serving provides approximately 310 kcal, 56 g of carbohydrate (predominantly from honey and milk sugars), 7.5 g of protein, and 7.5 g of fat. The glycemic load per serving is high (approximately 31), making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The beverage contains gluten from oats and dairy allergens. It is vegetarian and generally halal. Clinically, it should be treated as a high-sugar discretionary beverage. The oat component contributes a small amount of beta-glucan fibre, but the quantity per serving is insufficient to confer meaningful glycemic benefit. This drink is popular in the Taiwanese tea-shop culture that has expanded across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any approved primary source for this specific commercial product (CHICHA San Chen Oat Honey Milk Tea). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for brewed black tea, whole milk, oat milk, and honey combined in proportions typical of Taiwanese-style milk tea. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and honey. Confidence is medium because the recipe proportions are inferred and may vary by vendor and customisation.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimated composite beverage. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources at a level attributable to this beverage. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the component sources (brewed tea, oat milk, honey, and low-fat dairy). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA whole-milk and oat-milk component data. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 55 × available carbohydrate 56.0 g per 500 g serving) / 100 = 30.8. The food_name 'Oat Honey Milk Tea' is the canonical English rendering of the field survey listing 'Oat Honey Milk Tea燕麦NEW奶蜜茶'; the 'NEW' tag is a promotional label and has been excluded from the canonical name. Chinese local names use 燕麦蜂蜜奶茶 (oat honey milk tea) as the standard form; the input variant 奶蜜茶 (honey milk tea) is retained as an alias. Source match type is estimated; a future update should seek a direct HPB or USDA Branded Food entry if CHICHA San Chen publishes official nutrition data.
